text: Q: What does Beekes suggest about the origin of the word, and what is the original form proposed? A: Beekes suggests that the word has probably a Pre-Greek origin. The original form was probably the Mycenean Greek (Pot(s)eidawn). "The intervocalic aspiration suggests a Pre-Greek (Pelasgian) origin rather than an Indoeuropean one".<|endoftext|>
Q: What deity's name appears more frequently than di-u-ja ("Zeus") in the names listed? A: po-se-da-wo-ne and Po-se-da-o ("Poseidon")<|endoftext|>

Q: What title was Poseidon associated with in the Homeric era and classical Greece, and what additional meaning did it carry? A: Poseidon was identified with the title wanax from the Homeric era to classical Greece. The title didn't mean only king, but also protector.<|endoftext|>
Q: What was Po-tini-ja associated with in the Mycenean religion? A: Po-tini-ja was the Mycenean goddess of nature and Poseidon.<|endoftext|>
Q: What do Potnia and wanassa refer to according to the snippet? A: Potnia and wanassa refer to identical deities or two aspects of the same deity.<|endoftext|>

Q: What is the possible Linear B inscription related to Demeter, and is its interpretation certain? A: It is possible that Demeter appears as Da-ma-te in a Linear B inscription (PN EN 609), however the interpretation is still under dispute.<|endoftext|>

Q: What are the possible associations of "wa-na-soi" in the context of the tablets from Pylos? A: Wa-na-soi may be related with Demeter and Persephone, or their precursors, goddesses who were not associated with Poseidon in later periods.<|endoftext|>

Q: What was the cult title of Poseidon during the Mycenean period, and how was he worshipped in Knossos? A: A cult title of Poseidon was "earth-shaker" and in Knossos he was worshipped together with the goddess Eleithyia who was related to the annual birth of the divine child.<|endoftext|>

Q: What were Demeter and Poseidon considered in the cults at Thelpusa and Phigalia according to the Arcadian myths? A: In these cults Demeter and Poseidon were chthonic divinities of the underworld.<|endoftext|>

Q: What form did Poseidon take when he mated with Demeter during her wandering in search of her daughter? A: Poseidon took the form of a stallion and after their mating she gave birth to a daughter whose name was not allowed to be told to the unitiated and a horse called Arion (very swift).<|endoftext|>

Q: What did Zeus send to Demeter when the fruits of the earth were perished, and what did Demeter do in response? A: Zeus sent the Moirai to Demeter who listened to them and led aside her wrath.<|endoftext|>
Q: What did Demeter do as a sign of her sorrow according to the Homeric Hymn? A: Demeter puts a dark mourning robe around her shoulders as a sign of her sorrow.<|endoftext|>
Q: Where does Poseidon appear as a stallion and mate with Erinys near the spring of Tilpousa? A: At Haliartos in Boeotia near Thebes.<|endoftext|>
Q: What did Pegasus create when he stuck his hoof into the ground near Helikon? A: Pegasus stuck the ground with his hoof and created the famous spring Hippocrene near Helikon.<|endoftext|>

Q: What were Praxidicai and how were they depicted in images? A: Praxidicai were female deities of judicial punishment worshipped in the region of Haliartos in the historical times. Their images depicted only the heads of the goddesses probably a representation of the earth goddess emerging from the ground.<|endoftext|>

Q: What was Poseidon's title in the Mycenaean period, and what was the significance of his consort Potnia? A: At Pylos and some other cities he was a god of the underworld (Lord of the Underworld) and his cult was related to the protection of the palace. He carried the title anax, king or protector. His consort potnia, lady or mistress, was the Mycenean goddess of nature. Her main aspects were birth and vegetation.<|endoftext|>
Q: What does the horse represent in Greek mythology according to the snippet? A: The horse represents the divine spirit (numen) and is related to the liquid element and the underworld.<|endoftext|>

Q: What is the significance of Demeter-Erinys giving birth to Arion and an unnamable daughter who has the shape of a mare at Thelpousa? A: At Thelpousa Demeter-Erinys gives birth to Arion and to an unnamable daughter who has the shape of a mare.<|endoftext|>

Q: What did some ancient cults worship Poseidon as, and what were some of the horses associated with him in myth? A: In ancient cults Poseidon was worshipped as a horse. The horse Arion was a sire of Poseidon-horse with Erinys and the winged horse Pegasus a sire of Poseidon foaled by Medousa.<|endoftext|>
Q: What is the origin of all rivers and springs according to the snippet? A: The primeval water who encircled the earth (Oceanus) is the origin of all rivers and springs.<|endoftext|>

Q: What did Farnell suggest about Poseidon's original association and how did it relate to the myths of horses in Boeotia and Arcadia? A: Farnell suggested that Poseidon was originally the god of the Minyans who occupied Thessaly and Boeotia. There is a similarity between the Boeotian and Arcadian myths and especially between the myths which represent the god of the waters Poseidon as a horse. The mythical horse Arion appears in both regions.<|endoftext|>

Q: What did the Greeks believe was the cause of earthquakes according to their folk belief? A: The Greeks believed that the cause of the earthquakes was the erosion of the rocks by the waters, by the rivers in Peloponnese which they saw to disappear into the earth and then to burst out again.<|endoftext|>
Q: Who is described as a majestic, scary, and avenging monarch of the sea in Homer's Odyssey? A: Poseidon is described as a majestic, scary, and avenging monarch of the sea.<|endoftext|>
Q: In which region was Poseidon especially honoured according to the snippet? A: in the Peloponnese which is called "the residence of Poseidon" and in the Ionic cities.<|endoftext|>

Q: What was the name of the month associated with Poseidon in Ionic territories, Athens, the Aegean islands, and Asia Minor, and how was Poseidon worshipped during this month? A: The name of the month was used in Ionic territories, in Athens, in the islands of the Aegean and in the cities of Asia Minor. During this month Poseidon was worshipped as the "master of the sea" in a bright cult.<|endoftext|>
Q: What types of events were included in the fests of Poseidon? A: Many fests of Poseidon included athletic competitions and horseracing.<|endoftext|>
Q: What was a part of the athletic games in honour of the god in Onchestos of Boeotia? A: horseracing was a part of the athletic games in honour of the god.<|endoftext|>

Q: What was the significance of the festival of Panionia in relation to Poseidon? A: The Panionia, the festival of all Ionians near Mycale, were celebrated in honour of Poseidon Helikonios and was the place of meeting of the Ionian League.<|endoftext|>

Q: What roles does the deity play in relation to sailors and earthquakes? A: He is the protector of the sailors and has the ability to calm the sea for a good voyage and save those who are in danger. He is also the protector against the earthquakes.<|endoftext|>

Q: What was the god believed to have taught men, and how was he depicted? A: The god was believed to have taught men the art of taming horses. He was depicted on horseback, or riding in a chariot drawn by two or four horses.<|endoftext|>

Q: What was Poseidon associated with in terms of inland waters and vegetation? A: The god of inland waters is very close to vegetation and Poseidon was worshipped in many cities as god of vegetation.<|endoftext|>

Q: What animals were sacrificed to Poseidon in the Ionic festival (Panionia) according to Homer? A: The sacrifice of a bull offered to Poseidon is mentioned by Homer in an Ionic festival (Panionia).<|endoftext|>

Q: What did Alexander the Great do before the climactic battle of Issus according to the fragmentary papyrus? A: Alexander the Great paused at the Syrian seashore before the climactic battle of Issus, and resorted to prayers, "invoking Poseidon the sea-god, for whom he ordered a four-horse chariot to be cast into the waves".<|endoftext|>

Q: What did Apollo and Poseidon jointly oversee in the context of colonization according to the snippet? A: In colonization, Delphic Apollo provided the authorization to go out and settle, while Poseidon watched over the colonists on their way, and provided the lustral water for the foundation-sacrifice.<|endoftext|>
Q: What ancient text attributes epilepsy to Poseidon? A: A Hippocratic text of ca 400 BC, On the Sacred Disease says that he was blamed for certain types of epilepsy.<|endoftext|>

Q: What is the relationship between Poseidon and the oldest Greek god of the sea Pontus? A: Poseidon had a variety of roles, duties and attributes. He is a separate deity from the oldest Greek god of the sea Pontus.<|endoftext|>
Q: What is the title of Poseidon as king of the underworld in Mycenaean Greek (Linear B)? A: wa-na-ka<|endoftext|>

Q: What do some of the epithets like Enosigaios, Enosichthon, and Ennosidas mean, and what does this indicate about Zeus? A: Some of the epithets (or adjectives) applied to him like Enosigaios, Enosichthon (Homer) and Ennosidas (Pindar), mean "earth shaker". These epithets indicate his chthonic nature.<|endoftext|>

Q: What epithets does the god associated with earthquakes have, and where is his temple located? A: The god had the epithets Themeliouchos "upholding the foundations", Asphaleios "securer, protector" with a temple at Tainaron.<|endoftext|>
Q: What epithet does Homer use for Poseidon, and what does it mean? A: Homer uses for Poseidon the title Kyanochaites, "dark-haired, dark blue of the sea".<|endoftext|>
Q: What is one of Poseidon's epithets related to fishing, and where is it associated? A: His epithet Phykios, "god of seaweeds" at Mykonos, seems to be related with fishing.<|endoftext|>

Q: What epithet is Poseidon known by in Arcadia, and where are some of his temples located? A: He is known under the epithet Hippios, "of a horse or horses" usually in Arcadia. He had temples at Lycosura, Mantineia, Methydrium, Pheneos, Pallandion.<|endoftext|>
Q: What is the origin of the name Pegasus according to the snippet? A: He is the father of Pegasus, whose name is derived from (pg) "spring".<|endoftext|>

Q: What was the name of the festival at Ephesus in honour of Poseidon Taureios, and what does the epithet Taureios signify? A: At Ephesus there was a fest "Tavria" and he had the epithet Taureios, "related with the bull".<|endoftext|>

Q: What were the Panhellenic Isthmian Games celebrated in honour of, and what activities were included in the festival? A: The Panhellenic Isthmian Games were celebrated in honour of Poseidon. The festival included athletic and musical competitions and horseracing.<|endoftext|>

Q: What was the significance of Mycale in Ionia according to the snippet? A: Mycale was a promontory, between Samos and Miletus. The representatives of twelve cities (dodekapolis) celebrated the Panionia (of all the Ionians), a festival of Poseidon Helikonios. Traditionally the first settlers landed in this place. The temple became the meeting place of the Ionian League). Homer describes the sacrifice of a bull to Poseidon, during the festival.<|endoftext|>

Q: What festival was celebrated in honor of Poseidon Taureios in Ionia and what were the capbearers called? A: The fest Tauria was celebrated in honour of Poseidon Taureios and the capbearers were called tauroi (bulls).<|endoftext|>

Q: What was the patron god of the Amphictiony of Kalaureia and for whom was the festival celebrated? A: Poseidon was the patron god of the Amphictiony of Kalaureia. The festival was celebrated in honour of the god.<|endoftext|>

Q: What festival was celebrated in honour of Poseidon and who were the participants called? A: The famous festival Tainaria was celebrated in honour of Poseidon. The participants were called Tainarioi.<|endoftext|>

Q: What did the charioteers do at the beginning of the horseracing festival dedicated to Poseidon in Boeotia? A: At the beginning of the race the charioteers jumped down and made a prayer to Poseidon to protect them if the chariot would fall in the sacred grove.<|endoftext|>
Q: What event led to the destruction of the city of Helike in 370 BC? A: The city was destroyed by a tsunami in 370 BC.<|endoftext|>

Q: What was the surname of Poseidon worshipped in Mantineia in Arcadia, and what festival included the athletic games Hippokrateia? A: Poseidon was worshipped with the surname Hippios (of the horse). The fest included the athletic games Hippokrateia.<|endoftext|>
Q: What was Myconos associated with, and what restriction was placed on women during this festival? A: In a fest he was worshipped as a god of fishing and women were not allowed.<|endoftext|>

Q: What was the name of the great fest celebrated in Tinos in honour of Poseidon, and what role was Poseidon worshipped as? A: A great fest called Poseidonia was celebrated in honour of Poseidon. Poseidon was worshipped as a healer-god.<|endoftext|>

Q: What is considered a pioneering building featuring Doric architecture, and where was it located? A: A building constructed in early 7th century BC c.690-650 BC at Isthmia near Corinth which was later dedicated to Poseidon, is considered a pioneering building featuring Doric architecture.<|endoftext|>

Q: What is the characteristic of the distance between columns in Doric early work, and where can this be observed? A: In Doric early work the distance between column and column differs on the fronts and on the flanks and this can be observed in the temple of Poseidon at Kalaureia and in Basilica at Paestum.<|endoftext|>

Q: When was the temple dedicated to Poseidon in Isthmia likely built, and what architectural feature did it have? A: The temple dedicated later to the god Poseidon was probably built in early 7th century BC c.690-650 BC in the city Isthmia near Corinth and it had a wooden peristyle.<|endoftext|>

Q: What was traditionally believed to be the dedication of the 5th century BC temple at Paestum, and what do recent excavations suggest? A: Traditionally this name is associated with the 5th century BC temple at Paestum, however recent excavations indicate that both temples were dedicated to Hera.<|endoftext|>
Q: What deity was worshipped in the temple of Poseidon besides Zeus? A: another deity, whose identity is unknown.<|endoftext|>
Q: What were the estimated maximum dimensions of the temple of Poseidon at the stylobate in Taranto? A: The maximum dimensions of the temple at the stylobate could be 22,32X 47,46 m.<|endoftext|>
Q: What were the dimensions of the cella with porches and adyta in the first temple of Poseidon in Sounion? A: c.9.00 X21,20m<|endoftext|>
Q: What was the size of the cella with porches and adyta in the second temple? A: c.9.00 X21,20m<|endoftext|>

Q: What material was primarily used to construct the Doric temple of Poseidon on Kalaureia, and what were its dimensions at the stylobate? A: The Doric temple was constructed mainly of poros stone. It measured 14,50 X 27,00 m at the stylobate.<|endoftext|>
Q: What was the size of the temple of Poseidon at the stylobate? A: approximately 15,00 X30,00 m at the stylobate<|endoftext|>

Q: What was the Tainaron peninsula known for in terms of religious significance? A: The sacred sanctuary of Poseidon was built in a cave at the Tainaron peninsula. The path to the interior, carved into the rock, was preparing him who wanted to get into the psychopompeion. It also functioned as a necromancy and oneiromancy temple. The temple was also established as a place for persecuted who fled there for protection.<|endoftext|>

Q: What material was used to construct the temple of Poseidon and Amphitrite on Tinos, and what symbols were depicted on it? A: The temple was made of local marble and had some representations of the god's symbols, such as dolphins and the trident.<|endoftext|>
Q: What did Zeus, Poseidon, and Hades divide among themselves after defeating the Titans? A: Zeus got the sky, Poseidon the sea, and Hades the Underworld.<|endoftext|>

Q: How did Rhea save Poseidon from being devoured by Cronus in the rarer version of the myth? A: By offering Cronus a foal instead, claiming she had given birth to a horse instead of a god, while she had actually laid the child in a flock.<|endoftext|>
Q: Who became the nurse of Poseidon when he was given to the Telchines? A: Capheira, an Oceanid nymph, became the young god's nurse.<|endoftext|>
Q: What did Poseidon do to the island of Kos according to the snippet? A: Poseidon broke off a piece of the island of Kos called Nisyros, and threw it on top of Polybotes.<|endoftext|>

Q: What event involved the priests of Athena and the priest of Poseidon processing under canopies to Eleusis? A: The dissolution festival at the end of the year in the Athenian calendar, the Skira.<|endoftext|>

Q: What was Poseidon's gift to the Athenians according to the snippet? A: Poseidon struck the ground with his trident and a spring sprang up; the water was salty and not very useful, but represented his true gift - the access to trade.<|endoftext|>

Q: What did Athena offer to the Athenians, and why was it accepted? A: Athena offered an olive tree. The Athenians or their king, Cecrops, accepted the olive tree and along with it Athena as their patron, for the olive tree brought wood, oil and food.<|endoftext|>
Q: According to Burkert, what did Poseidon do in his anger at losing, leading to the death of Erectheus? A: Poseidon led his son Eumolpus against Athens and killed Erectheus.<|endoftext|>

Q: What happened to Halirrhothius when he swung his axe at Athena's tree gift, and how did this affect Poseidon and Ares? A: Halirrhothius missed his aim and it fell in himself, killing him instantly. Poseidon in fury accused Ares of murder, and the matter was eventually settled on the Areopagus ("hill of Ares") in favour of Ares, which was thereafter named after the event.

Q: What did Helios and Poseidon receive in the myth about the founding of Corinth according to the Hecatoncheires Briareos? A: Briareus decided to award the Acrocorinth to Helios, while to Poseidon he gave the isthmus of Corinth.<|endoftext|>
Q: What did Poseidon agree to give Leto in exchange for the island of Calauria? A: Poseidon agreed to give Leto the island of Delos.<|endoftext|>
Q: Who did Poseidon rescue from a satyr and subsequently father a child with? A: Poseidon rescued Amymone from a satyr and subsequently fathered a child with her.<|endoftext|>
Q: What elements were included in Theseus's origins according to the snippet? A: Theseus's origins included both the human and the divine element.<|endoftext|>

Q: Why did Minos sacrifice an ordinary bull instead of the splendid white bull offered by Poseidon? A: Wishing to keep the beautiful animal for himself, Minos instead sacrificed an ordinary bull to the sea-god instead of the agreed upon one.<|endoftext|>
Q: What creature was produced from Pasiphae's coupling with the bull, and what did it feed on? A: the Minotaur, a half-bull half-human creature who fed on human flesh.<|endoftext|>

Q: What did Theseus decide to do once he was recognized by Aegeus as his son? A: He decided to end the bloody tax Athens had to pay to Crete once and for all, and volunteered to set sail to Crete along with the other Athenian youths who had been chosen to be devoured by the Minotaur.<|endoftext|>

Q: What did Minos do to insult Theseus when Theseus arrived in Crete? A: Minos insulted Theseus and insisted he was no son of Poseidon; to demonstrate so, he threw his own ring in to the sea, and commanded Theseus to retrieve it, expecting he would not be able to do so.<|endoftext|>
Q: What did Theseus receive from the Nereid Amphitrite to prove his words? A: a purple wedding cloak and a crown<|endoftext|>
Q: What did Poseidon do when Theseus asked him to destroy Hippolytus? A: Poseidon sent a terrifying sea monster to spook the man's horses, which then dragged him to his death.<|endoftext|>
Q: What did Poseidon do in vengeance against King Laomedon after he failed to fulfill his promise of immortal horses? A: Poseidon sent a sea monster to attack Troy.<|endoftext|>
Q: Who was Poseidon's consort, and what was their child's name? A: Poseidon's consort was Amphitrite, and together they had a son named Triton.<|endoftext|>
Q: Who became the first ruler of Atlantis according to the snippet? A: the firstborn, Atlas, became the first ruler of Atlantis.<|endoftext|>
Q: What did Poseidon do to Cercyon's daughter after she was buried alive? A: Poseidon turned her into the local spring.<|endoftext|>
Q: Who were some of Poseidon's human children? A: Poseidon was thought to have fathered the famed Theseus, Bellerophon, Alebion and Bergion.<|endoftext|>
Q: What was the outcome of the love between Poseidon and Nerites? A: From the love between Poseidon and Nerites was born Anteros, mutual love.<|endoftext|>

Q: What happened when Poseidon pursued Demeter in the archaic myth? A: She spurned his advances, turning herself into a mare so that she could hide in a herd of horses; he saw through the deception and became a stallion, captured and raped her. Their child was a horse, Arion, which was capable of human speech.<|endoftext|>
Q: According to Ovid, what happened to Medusa after Poseidon seduced her? A: Athena, furious over the sacrilege, changed the beautiful girl into a monster.<|endoftext|>
Q: What did Caenis wish for after being raped by Poseidon? A: any wish.<|endoftext|>

Q: Who was the mortal woman married to Cretheus and who had a son named Aeson, and what river god did she love? A: A mortal woman named Tyro was married to Cretheus (with whom she had one son, Aeson), but loved Enipeus, a river god.<|endoftext|>
Q: What happened to Corone when Poseidon attempted to court her? A: Athena, witnessing all that, took pity in the girl and changed her into a crow.<|endoftext|>

Q: What does the snippet provide a list of? A: The following is a list of Poseidon's offspring, by various mothers. Beside each offspring, the earliest source to record the parentage is given, along with the century to which the source (in some cases approximately) dates.<|endoftext|>
